Han asked John, "What are your plans for the weekend?"

John replied, "I'm going to explore Harvard University. I'm planning to apply there after high school, so I want to learn more about it. What about you?"

"I spend Saturdays with my Christian brothers," John said.

"Wow, John, I didn't know you were religious. You seem more like a science enthusiast, like Leonard from 'The Big Bang Theory,'" Han exclaimed.

"Come on, Han, don't be dramatic. Many Americans, including scientists, believe in religion," John chuckled.

"But I'm surprised. I thought science and religion were incompatible," Han said.

"They can be compatible. In fact, the U.S. is both the most advanced and the most religious country in the world," John explained.

"I've heard that, but I don't understand how people believe in God when they know God doesn't exist," Han confessed.

"That's a bit complex. You'll understand better when you learn more about U.S. history. Religion has always been a part of American life. Even Harvard University, a highly academic institution, has religious roots," John said.

"Really?" Han asked, surprised.

"Absolutely! It was founded by Puritan immigrants from England," John confirmed.

"Why Puritans? Where were the non-religious educators?" Han inquired.

"Most early English immigrants to the U.S. were Puritans," John explained.

"But why were they so religious?" Han pressed.

"During the 16th and 17th centuries, there were religious reforms in England. Many religious separatists faced persecution and fled to other countries," John answered.

"So, religious freedom wasn't available in England?" Han deduced.

"Yes, that's why the Puritans came to the New World, hoping to establish a haven for themselves. Later, these Puritans founded Harvard College," John said.

"Why did they want to create a college?" Han wondered.

"Many Puritans had received a classical education at Oxford and Cambridge Universities in England. They wanted their descendants to have the same opportunities," John explained.

"I see," Han said.

"Harvard was initially called 'New College' without a formal name," John added.

"When did it become known as Harvard University?" Han asked.

"In 1639, it was renamed after its primary benefactor, John Harvard, a clergyman," John revealed.

"It seems that Harvard was destined to be influenced by religion," Han mused.

"It's not an exaggeration to say that without religion, there wouldn't be Harvard," John stated confidently.

"Wow, I had no idea religion played such a significant role in Harvard's history," Han exclaimed.

"Religion has been an integral part of Harvard's history, as well as American history," John affirmed.

"I'm starting to understand Harvard better. I realize my knowledge is limited. I have a lot to learn before applying," Han said.

"Remember, Harvard is part of the United States. To truly understand Harvard, you need to understand U.S. history and culture. They are deeply intertwined," John advised.

"That's right. I have much to learn from you," Han acknowledged.
